name: BYD K9M Electric
funFact: Utilizes proprietary iron-phosphate battery chemistry integrated directly into the chassis for an exceptionally low center of gravity.

name: Proterra Catalyst BE40
funFact: Metro's Proterra battery buses were retired unusually early after parts availability became a problem across multiple agencies.

name: New Flyer Xcelsior XE40
funFact: Metro's first XE40s entered service in 2022 after an inspection and testing program that began with unit 4700.

name: Solaris Urbino 12 Electric
funFact: King County Metro became the first North American buyer of a Solaris bus with its Urbino 12 Electric order.

name: Gillig Low Floor EV Plus
funFact: This became part of Metro's 2024-2025 battery-electric expansion, with the first buses entering service in early 2026.

name: Nova Bus LFSe+ Rapid
funFact: This battery-electric bus can recharge through overhead pantograph bursts at route endpoints instead of sitting idle for long depot charges.

name: New Flyer Xcelsior XE60
funFact: Metro's XE60 carries a 525 kWh battery pack and was advertised with a roughly 140-mile range.

name: Solaris Urbino 18 Electric
funFact: This 60-foot Solaris order gave Metro an unusually distinctive articulated electric bus option for the fleet.

name: MCI D45 CRT LE
funFact: Built for long-haul commuter duty, this low-entry coach blends highway range with easier boarding than traditional intercity buses.

name: Prevost H3-45
funFact: Standing at nearly 12 feet tall with an integrated theater-style floor configuration, it is the premier luxury touring charter coach in North America.

name: Van Hool Exqui.City (Articulated)
funFact: Often called a 'streetcar on rubber tires,' these articulated giants use optical guidance systems to help the driver dock perfectly at stations.

name: New Flyer Xcelsior XDE35
funFact: This order was transferred to New Flyer after the Orion factory closed, and its Series-E system shuts the diesel engine off while stopped.

name: New Flyer Xcelsior XT40
funFact: Metro's XT40 fleet was its first low-floor trolleybus line with air conditioning and batteries for short off-wire operation.

name: Gillig Low Floor (29-foot)
funFact: Known for its 'kneeling' feature, this bus can lower its front suspension to the curb to allow easier boarding for passengers with limited mobility.

name: New Flyer Xcelsior XDE40
funFact: The Series-E HybriDrive system allows the diesel engine to shut down completely while stopped or at low speeds to drastically reduce emissions.

name: Alexander Dennis Enviro500
funFact: Double-decker coaches like this one move huge suburban crowds while still fitting inside the footprint of a standard single bus bay.

name: Superbus Concept
funFact: Designed in the Netherlands, this experimental aerodynamic electric high-speed vehicle features 16 gull-wing doors for direct cabin access.

name: Twin Coach GWFT
funFact: Originally built in 1940, this preserved Seattle trolleybus was restored to operating condition and later returned to its original fleet number, 905.

name: Orion VII Hybrid
funFact: These were the first Metro buses equipped with series diesel-electric hybrid propulsion.

name: New Flyer Xcelsior XDE60 (RapidRide G)
funFact: Metro's RapidRide G buses have five doors, including two on the left side, plus interior bike racks.

name: GM New Look 'Fishbowl'
funFact: Introduced in 1959, its iconic six-piece curved windshield earned it the 'Fishbowl' moniker, becoming the definitive North American transit bus for decades.

name: Greyhound MC-12 'Americruiser'
funFact: A historic interstate icon built by MCI for Greyhound in the 1980s, designed for cross-country performance on the newly constructed US interstate system.

name: New Flyer Xcelsior XT60
funFact: This articulated trolleybus combines wire power with battery flexibility, giving Metro a modern 60-foot electric workhorse.

name: New Flyer DE60LFA (RapidRide Prototype)
funFact: King County Metro's unit 6000 was a RapidRide prototype with a rear window, a feature dropped from the later production coaches.

name: Breda DuoBus 350
funFact: Seattle's Breda DuoBus fleet could run as both a diesel bus and an electric trolleybus, making it one of Metro's most unusual vehicle types.

name: MAN SG-T 310
funFact: These were part of the first fleet of articulated trolleybuses to operate in the United States.

name: CRRC Autonomous Rail Rapid Transit (ART)
funFact: A multi-articulated vehicle that follows virtual tracks painted directly on asphalt using advanced optical and radar sensors, behaving like a trackless train.

name: Neoplan Jumbocruiser
funFact: The only production articulated double-decker bus ever built. It features a unique walk-through bellows structure on the upper deck.

name: Volvo 7900 Electric Articulated
funFact: Features ultra-high-capacity layouts with dual-driven axles to maintain traction in extreme winter conditions across Europe.

name: AutoTram Extra Grand
funFact: Developed in Germany, this massive 100-foot multi-articulated vehicle is officially recognized as the world's longest passenger transit bus.
